title: Mapping Sequences to Sequences
concept: Seq2Seq maps an input sequence (e.g. English text) to an output sequence of a different length (e.g. French text).

exercises.prompt: What does a Seq2Seq model map?

options.text: An input sequence to an output sequence
A single image to a single number
A single word to a yes/no label

exercises.explain: Seq2Seq maps an input sequence (like text) to an output sequence (like translated text) of a potentially different length.

cards.title: Lost in Translation?
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how computers translate whole sentences from one language to another, even when the word order and sentence lengths are completely different? Let's discover how Seq2Seq models make this magic happen!

cards.title: What is Seq2Seq?
cards.body: Seq2Seq (Sequence-to-Sequence) is a type of AI model designed to take in one sequence of data (like a sentence in English) and transform it into another sequence of data (like that same sentence in French). Crucially, the input and output sequences don't have to be the same length!

cards.title: The Professional Translator
cards.body: Think of Seq2Seq like a professional translator. Instead of translating word-for-word as you speak, they listen to your entire English sentence, understand the meaning, and then write out the complete translation in French, even if it uses a different number of words!

cards.title: Language Translation
cards.body: When you type 'How are you?' (3 words) into Google Translate, the Seq2Seq AI might output 'Comment ça va ?' (3 words) or 'Comment allez-vous ?' (2 words). The AI successfully maps the meaning from one sequence length to another.

cards.title: Word-by-Word Myth
cards.body: Many people think machine translation just swaps words one-by-one. But that would make 'How are you?' sound gibberish in other languages! Seq2Seq models look at the entire sentence context to generate a natural-sounding output sequence.

cards.title: Word-for-Word vs. Seq2Seq
cards.body: Unlike old-school word-for-word translation, Seq2Seq reads the whole input sequence before creating the output sequence.

left.text: Translates each word individually without looking at the full sentence.

right.text: Processes the entire input sequence first, then outputs a complete, natural sequence.

exercises.prompt: What does the term 'Seq2Seq' stand for?
exercises.explain: Seq2Seq stands for Sequence-to-Sequence, which describes mapping an input sequence to an output sequence.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A Seq2Seq model requires the input sequence and the output sequence to have the exact same number of words.
exercises.explain: Seq2Seq models are highly flexible and are specifically designed to handle inputs and outputs of different lengths.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the core feature of Seq2Seq models:
exercises.sentence: Seq2Seq models are powerful because the input and output sequences can have [blank] lengths.
exercises.explain: In Seq2Seq tasks, the output sequence (like translated text) can have a different length than the input sequence.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'I want to use Seq2Seq to translate English to Spanish.' How should the AI agent respond?

options.text: AI Agent: 'Correct! Seq2Seq is great here because the English input and Spanish output can have different lengths.'
AI Agent: 'Wrong, Seq2Seq only works if the English and Spanish sentences have the exact same number of words.'
AI Agent: 'Incorrect, Seq2Seq cannot be used for translation at all.'

exercises.explain: The response generated by the AI agent is correct because Seq2Seq models are designed specifically to handle input and output sequences that have different lengths.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are designing a chatbot that needs to use a Seq2Seq model. Which of these tasks is the best fit for Seq2Seq?

options.text: Predicting the next word in an email search bar
Translating a paragraph from English to Spanish
Tagging a single photo as 'Cat' or 'Dog'
Counting the total number of words in a document

exercises.explain: Seq2Seq models are designed for sequence-to-sequence tasks where the input and output can have different lengths. Translating a sentence is a classic example of this, whereas predicting the next word or classifying an email does not map a full sequence to another sequence of arbitrary length.
